context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m kind of lost on my reflection for the urban bias thesis prompt. Can you just explain the whole topic from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through this! Before we dive in, tell me: what do you already know or remember about the urban bias thesis from your readings? Even a rough idea is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole topic, which would essentially do the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and guide them to articulate what they already know. I'll start with a simple, open-ended question to assess their baseline understanding and get them engaged with the material.

exchanges.student: Honestly not much. I just know it has something to do with cities getting more attention than rural areas, but then I also get confused about tradeoffs, linkages, and the industrial growth part too. Can you maybe just give me the full picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, that's a solid starting point - cities getting more attention than rural areas is the core intuition behind the thesis. So let me ask you: *why* might a government prioritize cities over rural areas? What would motivate that choice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain everything, which I should not do. However, they've actually given me something to work with - they have a basic intuition about the urban bias thesis. I should affirm that starting point and use it as a springboard for a Socratic question that pushes them to develop their thinking further, rather than providing a full explanation.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, maybe jobs or money or politics? I feel like I’m guessing and I don’t know which part matters most. I think I need the big overview before I can answer stuff like that.
exchanges.tutor: Those aren't guesses - jobs, money, and politics are exactly the kinds of factors scholars point to. Pick one that feels most intuitive to you and tell me: how do you think it leads a government to favor cities over rural areas?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking for a full overview, but their guesses (jobs, money, politics) are actually quite good and touch on real reasons. I should validate these instincts and push them to develop one of these threads rather than giving an overview. I'll pick the most productive thread and ask a focused follow-up to build momentum.

exchanges.student: Maybe politics, but then I also feel like it could be industry, migration, or just colonial history, so now I’m confused again. I’m kind of mixing everything together — could you just walk me through the whole argument from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through it all - that's your job as the writer! But notice what just happened: you independently connected politics, industry, migration, and colonial history to urban bias. That's real thinking. Let's narrow down: your reflection has three parts to address - the urban bias thesis, urban vs. rural linkages, and industrial growth. Which of the three feels most approachable to start with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for a full explanation, which I must refuse. However, they're actually demonstrating good instincts by connecting politics, industry, migration, and colonial history - these are all genuinely relevant to the prompt. The issue is they feel overwhelmed by complexity. I should acknowledge the connections they're making are valid, but redirect them to focus on one thread to build confidence rather than trying to grasp everything at once. I should be firm about not providing the overview.

exchanges.student: Honestly none of them feels approachable on its own because they all blur together for me. I think if I heard a simple beginning-to-end explanation once, I could probably separate them after that.
exchanges.tutor: I hear your frustration, but walking you through it isn't my role - and honestly, it won't help you write the reflection either. That understanding needs to come from your readings. Have you had a chance to read the assigned material on urban-rural linkages? What's one thing - even one sentence or idea - that stuck with you from those readings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for a full explanation - this is now the fourth time. I need to be firm and clear about my role while still being supportive. I should redirect them to their readings, which are the actual source material, and then try a different angle to get them engaging with the content. Rather than asking about concepts, I might ask something more concrete and grounded that gets them moving.

exchanges.student: I read it, but I can’t really pull out one clean idea. I just remember something about cities depending on rural areas and rural areas depending on cities, but then I don’t know how that fits with urban bias or whether I’m supposed to argue for urban development or rural development.
exchanges.tutor: That interdependence idea is actually quite useful - hold onto it. But notice the prompt doesn't ask you to pick a winner between urban and rural. It asks what we should make of the urban bias thesis, and about linkages *between* the two. So here's a question: if cities and rural areas depend on each other, what does that suggest about a government that only invests in one side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually pulled out a meaningful idea - the interdependence between cities and rural areas - which is directly relevant to the 'linkages' part of the prompt. I should validate this and use it as a springboard. I also notice the student is misreading the prompt slightly - they think they need to argue FOR one side, when the prompt is more nuanced. I should gently correct this framing and push them to think about what 'linkages' means in relation to urban bias.

exchanges.student: I guess it means that could backfire, but I’m still not sure how to turn that into the reflection. I’m also still fuzzy on the industrial growth and class formation part, so I feel like I’m missing the whole structure.
exchanges.tutor: "It could backfire" is actually a strong argumentative move - that's the kind of claim a reflection can be built around. You have more than you think. On industrial growth and class formation: when industries grow in cities, who do you think moves there, and what happens to the people left behind in rural areas?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good inference ('it could backfire') which I should affirm. They're now asking about industrial growth and class formation, which is the third part of the prompt. Rather than explaining it, I should push them to reason through it themselves using what they already know. I also notice we've been circling the same territory for several exchanges - the student keeps feeling like they're missing the full picture before they can write. I should encourage them to start building their argument from what they have, rather than waiting for complete clarity.

exchanges.student: Maybe workers move to cities for factory jobs, and then rural areas lose people or change somehow? But then I’m not sure if that’s good, bad, or both, and I still feel like I need the whole thing tied together instead of these separate little pieces.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built the skeleton of your argument across our conversation: urban bias can backfire because of urban-rural interdependence, industrial growth pulls workers to cities, and rural areas are transformed in the process. That's not 'separate little pieces' - that's a coherent thread. Rather than keep talking about it, try writing a few sentences connecting these ideas. What would your opening argument look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently generated three solid ideas: urban bias can backfire due to interdependence, industrial growth draws workers to cities, and rural areas are affected by that migration. These are the building blocks of their reflection. We've been circling for several exchanges - it's time to push them to integrate these ideas into written content rather than continuing to discuss pieces in the abstract. Per the rules, after 3 messages on similar territory I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: I’m still blanking on how to word the opening. Could you at least give me a sample overview paragraph so I can see how all the pieces connect?
exchanges.tutor: Writing a sample paragraph for you isn't something I'll do - the writing has to be yours. Imperfect is fine to start. Just try one sentence: what is the urban bias thesis, in your own words, based on what we've discussed? Don't worry about it being polished.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse - this is a clear boundary. However, I should acknowledge their progress and redirect them to attempt writing something imperfect rather than waiting for a model. I should be direct that writing samples are not something I provide, and push them to just try, even messily.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s the idea that development policy often favors cities over rural areas, but I still feel really unsure and like I’m missing half of the concept. Can you just explain the concept more broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: That sentence is a solid, accurate definition of the urban bias thesis - you're not missing it, you just stated it clearly. Stop waiting for permission to start writing. Now add one more sentence: what's the risk of that bias, based on what you said earlier about urban-rural interdependence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just written a perfectly valid one-sentence definition of the urban bias thesis. I should strongly affirm this and point out that they are not missing half the concept - they have it. This is also the fifth or sixth time they've asked for a broad explanation, so I need to be firm and redirect them toward building on what they have rather than continuing to ask for an overview. I should push them to write more from this starting point.
